steps.title: Identify what evidence supports Browner's claim
steps.content: Browner claims Ballard's compositions rely on elements originating within Native musical traditions. To support this, the correct answer must (1) state Browner's claim and (2) pair it with concrete evidence from the notes. Only one note provides that evidence: Devil’s Promenade incorporates a Seneca cow-horn rattle, a traditional Native instrument.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘According to Browner, composer Louis Ballard, who wrote Desert Trilogy, relies on elements with origins in Native musical traditions.’: States Browner's claim but pairs it with Desert Trilogy, which has no noted Native musical elements. The wrong composition is cited as evidence. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Classical composer Louis Ballard, who wrote Devil’s Promenade, was nominated for the Pulitzer Prize in music in 1971.’: Omits Browner's claim entirely and states a factual error (it was Desert Trilogy, not Devil’s Promenade, that was nominated). Neither supports the claim. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Browner posits that Louis Ballard’s compositions rely on elements originating within Native musical traditions; Devil’s Promenade affirms this assertion, incorporating a Seneca cow-horn rattle.’: States Browner's claim and provides the specific evidence from the notes that confirms it — Devil’s Promenade with its Seneca cow-horn rattle. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘Browner claims that Louis Ballard’s compositions, one of which was nominated for the Pulitzer Prize, rely on elements originating within Native musical traditions.’: States Browner's claim but inserts the Pulitzer Prize nomination, which is irrelevant to Native musical traditions and provides no supporting evidence. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
